extra.reasoning:
The only rule that sets rec_drug_a := true is r3, which requires all 10 conditions true (asthma ∧ g6pd_deficiency ∧ immunosuppressed ∧ low_ejection_fraction ∧ hyperkalemia ∧ diabetes ∧ seizure_history ∧ peptic_ulcer ∧ ckd_dialysis ∧ prior_gi_bleed). There is no other rule producing rec_drug_a, so rec_drug_a = true implies all 10 conditions hold.

For rec_drug_b, two rules can match: r1 (priority 50, IF asthma THEN rec_drug_b := true) and r5 (priority 90, IF all 10 conditions THEN rec_drug_b := false). When all 10 conditions are true, both r1 and r5 match. Conflict resolution selects the highest-priority matching rule per output; r5's priority 90 beats r1's priority 50, so rec_drug_b := false.

Thus in the only scenario where rec_drug_a can be true (all 10 conditions true), r5 forces rec_drug_b := false. Therefore rec_drug_a ∧ rec_drug_b can never be jointly true. In every other input, rec_drug_a is false (default), so the conjunction is trivially false. The high-priority suppression rule r5 was clearly designed to guarantee this mutual exclusion. The property holds across the entire input space.
